nativeRange.habitat: Found close to the bottom on sandy or rubble-strewn substrates near reefs, where it excavates burrows.

diet.notes: The Oreni Tilefish tends to accept a variety of meaty foods, including marine flesh, crustacean flesh, and frozen Mysis shrimp. Feeding two to three times per day is commonly advised.

avoidWith: large predatory fish that may consume it

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Jokuyken15, 5280x3520. Tack-sharp aquarium shot of a live, vibrant specimen showing the full elongated tilefish body, electric-blue lateral scribbling, dark upper saddle and gold dorsal; whole fish in frame on a sand bed.

summary: The Oreni Tilefish (Hoplolatilus oreni) is an exceptionally rare species from the Western Indian Ocean and Red Sea, known for burrowing into sandy substrate. It is generally considered peaceful and reef-compatible, making it a prized find for experienced marine aquarists.
careNotes:
The Oreni Tilefish is regarded as one of the rarest and most desirable tilefish available in the aquarium trade, sometimes referred to as the 'Holy Grail' of tilefish keeping. In the aquarium, it tends to live close to the bottom and will excavate burrows or construct mounds in the substrate, retreating into them when threatened — a deep sand bed of at least 7 to 10 cm is strongly advisable to accommodate this behavior. Unlike many of its relatives, this species has been reported to display considerably more personality and is generally considered less shy and skittish than other tilefish in the genus. Because it is an active fish capable of jumping, a tight-fitting aquarium cover is strongly recommended to prevent escapes.

Specimens available in the aquarium trade are most commonly sourced from Kenya. The species was originally described from a single specimen collected at Massawa, Ethiopia, in the Red Sea in 1973, and for decades was known only from that holotype. In 2018, the first confirmed live sighting was recorded at Sodwana Bay, South Africa, at approximately 47 metres depth, establishing that the species ranges along the full length of the East African coast — a distribution that explains the Kenya trade sourcing.

Because of the species' extreme rarity in the wild and in the trade, captive husbandry data remains very limited. Care parameters are largely extrapolated from close relatives within the genus Hoplolatilus.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Confirmed same data as primary URL; consistent on size (8 in), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), tank size (50 gal), care level (moderate), and reef compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Malacanthidae), adult size (14.1 cm SL vs LiveAquaria 8 in / 20.3 cm TL — measurements are different standards, not a true conflict), and native range. FishBase holotype locality is Massawa, Ethiopia (Red Sea); FishBase lists the species as 'known only from the holotype.' A 2018 ReefBuilders article (first live photo, Sodwana Bay, South Africa, ~47 m depth) establishes the range extension along the full East African coast, reconciling the Kenya trade sourcing with the Red Sea holotype. South Africa added to nativeRange.countries.

additional.name: ReefBuilders — First Live Picture of Hoplolatilus oreni
additional.notes: Key range-extension record: first live photograph taken at Sodwana Bay, South Africa (~47 m depth, 2018). Previously known only from Red Sea holotype; this sighting confirms presence along the East African coast and explains Kenya trade sourcing. No husbandry data in article.
